topics.title: Explore and develop interactive web pages using markup languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNL507 - WEB PROGRAMMING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Explore and develop interactive web pages using markup languages ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Markup Language** is a system of annotating a document in a syntactically distinguishable way from the text, using tags enclosed within angle brackets `< >`. In web engineering, **HTML (HyperText Markup Language)** is the foundational markup language used to structure and present content on the World Wide Web. The current standard, **HTML5** (W3C Recommendation, October 2014), introduces semantic elements, native multimedia support, and form controls that enable the development of **interactive web pages** — pages that respond to user actions (clicks, inputs, navigation) without requiring a full page reload. An **interactive web page** is a document whose Document Object Model (DOM) reacts dynamically to user events (`click`, `submit`, `hover`, `keypress`, etc.), with markup-level interactivity provided by HTML elements such as ``, ` ``` **Validation attributes used (highlighted for the examiner):** 1. `required` — on name, regno, email, branch, semester. 2. `pattern="[A-Z]{2}\d{8}"` — enforces register number format. 3. `type="email"` — built-in email format validation. 4. `minlength="3"`, `maxlength="50"` — length constraints. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** What are **semantic HTML5 elements**? Explain the roles of `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`, `
`, and `
` with one example each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Design an HTML5 page that displays a **student grade table** with columns: Register No, Name, Subject, Marks (out of 100), and Grade. Include a ``, ``, ``, and apply the `colspan` attribute in one row to show a remark. Also embed a video element with controls. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** **Semantic HTML5 elements** are tags that clearly describe the *meaning* of their content to both the browser and the developer, as opposed to generic containers like `
` and ``. | Element | Role | Example Use | |---------|------|-------------| | `
` | Introductory content or navigation for a page or section | `

KTU

` | | `
` | Major navigation links | `
  • Home
` | | `
` | Dominant content of the body (only one per page) | `
...
` | | `
` | Self-contained, distributable content | `

News item

` | | `
` | Thematic grouping of content with a heading | `

Chapter 1

` | | `
` | Tangentially related content (sidebars) | `
Related links
` | | `
` | Closing content for a page or section | `
© 2025 KTU
` | **Model Solution (b):** ```html Student Grade Report

KTU Semester Grade Report

End-Semester Results - 2025
Register No Name Subject Marks (out of 100) Grade
KT22CS001 Arjun Krishna Web Programming 92 A+
KT22CS002 Meera Nair Web Programming 85 A
Class Average: 88.5 | Topper: Arjun Krishna

Orientation Video

``` --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Experiment 2:** > 1. **Missing ``** — pages enter Quirks Mode; **lose 1–2 marks**. > 2. **Missing `
  • `, `` — **lose 0.5 mark per error**. > 5. **Forgetting `alt` attribute** on `` — accessibility fail; **lose 1 mark**. > 6. **Using `|` (raw pipe) in code displayed in text** — fine in HTML, but ensure the rendered output is clear. In exam answer sheets, **prefer the words "or" / "and"** to avoid ambiguity. > 7. **Not specifying the `method`** in `
    ` — assume default `GET`, but **state it explicitly** in answers. > 8. **Omitting `required`** on mandatory form fields — examiner will deduct marks for not demonstrating HTML5 validation. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Experiment 2** - ✅ Every HTML5 page starts with `` on line 1. - ✅ Always include `` and `` in the ``. - ✅ Use **semantic landmarks** (`
    `, `
    `, `
    `, `
    `) instead of `
    ` soup. - ✅ There should be **only one `

    ` per page** for SEO. - ✅ **Forms** must use `

topics.title: Implement client-side validation using scripting languages using Javascript and PHP
Design and develop web pages and implement databases connectivity using PHP and MySQL
topics.content:
# Module Experiment 2: Design and Develop Web Pages and Implement Database Connectivity using PHP and MySQL > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNL507 - WEB PROGRAMMING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Design and develop web pages and implement databases connectivity using PHP and MySQL --- ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **PHP (PHP: Hypertext Preprocessor)** is a **server-side, open-source, general-purpose scripting language** that is embedded within HTML and is specifically suited for web development. PHP scripts execute on the web server, generating dynamic HTML content that is sent to the client's browser. **MySQL** is an **open-source Relational Database Management System (RDBMS)** that uses **Structured Query Language (SQL)** to manage, store, retrieve, and manipulate data organized in tables with rows (records) and columns (fields). **Database Connectivity in PHP** refers to the process of establishing a communication channel between a PHP application and a MySQL database server using APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) such as **MySQLi (MySQL Improved)** or **PDO (PHP Data Objects)** to perform operations like Create, Read, Update, and Delete (CRUD). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A student must be able to design responsive web pages using **HTML, CSS, JavaScript**, handle user input through **PHP form processing**, and perform **persistent data storage/retrieval** using **MySQL** with appropriate connectivity techniques. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Think of a **restaurant** to understand this entire ecosystem: | Component | Real-World Analogy | Technical Counterpart | |-----------|-------------------|----------------------| | **Customer (Diner)** | Person who places an order | **Web Browser (Client)** | | **Waiter** | Takes order to kitchen & serves food | **Web Server (Apache/XAMPP)** | | **Chef** | Prepares the dish based on order | **PHP Engine** | | **Refrigerator/Pantry** | Stores ingredients (data) | **MySQL Database** | | **Menu Card** | Lists what can be ordered | **HTML Web Page / Form** | | **Order Slip** | Carries the request to chef | **HTTP Request (GET/POST)** | | **Served Dish** | Final output to customer | **HTTP Response (HTML page)** | **Workflow Intuition:** When a user fills a registration form on a webpage and clicks *Submit*, the data travels as an HTTP request → Web Server receives it → PHP script executes → PHP connects to MySQL → SQL query is executed (e.g., `INSERT`) → Database stores the record → PHP sends a success/failure response → Browser displays the result. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** PHP and MySQL work **together but separately**. PHP is the *brain* (logic), MySQL is the *memory* (storage). The connection is established only when PHP explicitly opens a channel using credentials (`host`, `username`, `password`, `database`). --- ### 1.3 Standard WAMP/MAMP/XAMPP Stack | Stack Element | Software | Role | Standard Port | |---------------|----------|------|---------------| | **W** | **Windows** | Operating System | — | | **A** | **Apache** | Web Server | **80** (HTTP), **443** (HTTPS) | | **M** | **MySQL / MariaDB** | Database Server | **3306** | | **P** | **PHP** | Server-Side Scripting | — | > [!TIP] > **Localhost URL convention:** `http://localhost//` — the **document root** for XAMPP is `C:\xampp\htdocs\` (Windows) and `/Applications/XAMPP/htdocs/` (macOS). --- ### 1.4 Two Main Connectivity APIs in PHP 1. **MySQLi (MySQL Improved Extension)** — Object-oriented and Procedural. MySQL-specific. 2. **PDO (PHP Data Objects)** — Object-oriented, supports **12+ database drivers** (MySQL, PostgreSQL, SQLite, etc.). **Recommended for portability.** Both support **Prepared Statements** which are the **industry-standard defense against SQL Injection attacks**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 PHP File Handling & Form Processing Logic ### 2.1.1 Superglobal Variables (Predefined Arrays) PHP provides built-in **superglobal arrays** accessible in every scope without declaration: | Superglobal | Purpose | HTTP Method | Visibility in URL | |-------------|---------|-------------|-------------------| | `$_GET` | Collects data from URL parameters | GET | **Yes** (visible) | | `$_POST` | Collects data from HTML form | POST | No (in body) | | `$_REQUEST` | Combines `$_GET`, `$_POST`, `$_COOKIE` | Both | Depends | | `$_SERVER` | Server & execution environment info | — | — | | `$_SESSION` | Stores user data across pages | — | — | | `$_COOKIE` | Stores small data on client browser | — | — | | `$_FILES` | Handles file uploads | POST (multipart) | — | | `$_ENV` | Environment variables | — | — | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** `$_GET` has a **URL length limit (~2048 chars)** and is insecure for passwords. Always use `$_POST` for sensitive data like passwords and personal information. ### 2.1.2 HTTP Request Methods | Method | Use Case | Idempotent? | Data Limit | Cacheable | |--------|----------|-------------|------------|-----------| | **GET** | Retrieve data, search queries | ✅ Yes | ~2048 chars | ✅ Yes | | **POST** | Submit forms, file upload, login | ❌ No | 8 MB (configurable) | ❌ No | > **Idempotent** means performing the operation multiple times produces the same result (e.g., viewing a page 5 times = 1 view). --- ## 2.2 MySQL Connection Architecture ### 2.2.1 Connection Parameters (Mandatory) Every PHP-MySQL connection requires these **4 critical parameters**: $$C = f(\text{host}, \text{username}, \text{password}, \text{database})$$ | Parameter | Default Value (Local XAMPP) | Description | |-----------|------------------------------|-------------| | `$host` | `localhost` or `127.0.0.1` | Server address (uses **TCP/IP port 3306**) | | `$user` | `root` | MySQL username | | `$pass` | *(empty string)* | MySQL password | | `$dbname` | (your database name) | Target database schema | ### 2.2.2 Connection Flow Diagram (Logical) $$\text{Client Browser} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP Request}} \text{Apache} \xrightarrow{\text{PHP Engine}} \text{mysqli\_connect()} \xrightarrow{\text{TPC Socket 3306}} \text{MySQL Server}$$ $$\text{MySQL Server} \xrightarrow{\text{Result Set}} \text{PHP Engine} \xrightarrow{\text{HTML}} \text{Apache} \xrightarrow{\text{HTTP Response}} \text{Client Browser}$$ --- ## 2.3 CRUD Operations — The Core of Database Interactivity CRUD is the **fundamental data manipulation model** used in virtually every database-backed application. | Operation | SQL Verb | MySQLi Method | Real-World Example | |-----------|----------|---------------|---------------------| | **C**reate | `INSERT INTO` | `mysqli_query($conn, $sql)` | Registering a new user | | **R**ead | `SELECT` | `mysqli_query()` + `mysqli_fetch_assoc()` | Displaying a product list | | **U**pdate | `UPDATE` | `mysqli_query($conn, $sql)` | Editing profile information | | **D**elete | `DELETE FROM` | `mysqli_query($conn, $sql)` | Removing an account | --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these for any 14-mark PHP-MySQL question on the KTU board exam.** ### 2.4.1 Connection Formulas | Operation | MySQLi Procedural | PDO (Object-Oriented) | |-----------|-------------------|----------------------| | **Connect** | `$conn = mysqli_connect($h, $u, $p, $d);` | `$conn = new PDO("mysql:host=$h;dbname=$d", $u, $p);` | | **Check Error** | `if(!$conn) die("Error: " . mysqli_connect_error());` | `try { ... } catch(PDOException $e) { echo $e->getMessage(); }` | | **Close** | `mysqli_close($conn);` | `$conn = null;` | ### 2.4.2 SQL Query Pattern Formulas | Task | SQL Template | |------|--------------| | **Insert** | `INSERT INTO table (col1, col2) VALUES ('$v1', '$v2')` | | **Select All** | `SELECT * FROM table` | | **Conditional Select** | `SELECT * FROM table WHERE id = $id` | | **Update** | `UPDATE table SET col1='$v' WHERE id=$id` | | **Delete** | `DELETE FROM table WHERE id=$id` | | **Aggregate Count** | `SELECT COUNT(*) AS total FROM table` | | **Login Check** | `SELECT * FROM users WHERE username='$u' AND password='$p'` | ### 2.4.3 Prepared Statement Formula (Security) **MySQLi:** ```php $stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ? AND password = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("ss", $email, $password); $stmt->execute(); $result = $stmt->get_result(); ``` **PDO:** ```php $stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = :email"); $stmt->execute([':email' => $email]); ``` ### 2.4.4 Status Check Formula | Function | Returns | Use | |----------|---------|-----| | `mysqli_affected_rows($conn)` | Number of affected rows | Verify INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE success | | `mysqli_num_rows($result)` | Number of rows in result | Verify SELECT returned data | | `mysqli_fetch_assoc($result)` | One row as associative array | Iterate through result set | | `mysqli_insert_id($conn)` | Last AUTO_INCREMENT ID | Get newly created record's ID | --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry Domain | PHP-MySQL Application | |-----------------|----------------------| | **E-Commerce** | Product catalog, shopping cart, order management (Amazon-like) | | **Education** | Student Information Systems, LMS, KTU ERP portals | | **Healthcare** | Patient records, appointment booking, prescription storage | | **Banking & Finance** | Account management, transaction history (with enhanced security) | | **Content Management** | WordPress (43% of all websites!), Drupal, Joomla | | **Social Media** | User profiles, posts, comments, likes | > **Famous platforms using PHP + MySQL:** **Facebook (originally), WordPress, Wikipedia, Slack, Mailchimp, Etsy.** # 3. Step-by-Step Code Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Lab Context:** The following code is a complete, runnable example of a **Student Registration & Login System** using PHP + MySQL — a typical KTU 14-mark lab experiment. --- ## 3.1 STEP 1 — Database & Table Creation (SQL) ```sql CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktu_lab; USE ktu_lab; CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS students ( id INT(6) UNSIGNED AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, reg_no VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL UNIQUE, name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, email VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE, password VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, department VARCHAR(50), created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); ``` **Logic:** - `AUTO_INCREMENT` → `id` is generated automatically starting from 1. - `PRIMARY KEY` → Uniquely identifies each row. - `UNIQUE` → Prevents duplicate registrations. - `VARCHAR(255)` for password → Accommodates **bcrypt-hashed** passwords (60 chars, but future-proof). --- ## 3.2 STEP 2 — Database Connection File (`db.php`) ```php connect_error) { die("Connection failed: " . $conn->connect_error); } echo "Connected successfully to KTU Lab Database"; ?> ``` **Output:** `Connected successfully to KTU Lab Database` **Why `die()`?** It terminates the script immediately, preventing execution of subsequent code with a broken connection — preventing **NullPointer-like errors** in PHP. --- ## 3.3 STEP 3 — Registration HTML Form (`register.html`) ```html KTU Student Registration

KTU Student Registration

``` **Key Concepts:** - `action="register.php"` → Form data is sent to `register.php` on submit. - `method="POST"` → Data sent in HTTP body (not URL). - `required` → HTML5 client-side validation. - `type="email"` → Browser validates email format. --- ## 3.4 STEP 4 — Registration Processing (`register.php`) ```php query($check); if ($result->num_rows > 0) { echo "Error: Email already registered!"; } else { // 3. Insert new student record $sql = "INSERT INTO students (reg_no, name, email, password, department) VALUES ('$reg_no', '$name', '$email', '$password', '$department')"; if ($conn->query($sql) === TRUE) { echo "Registration successful! Welcome, " . htmlspecialchars($name); } else { echo "Error: " . $conn->error; } } } $conn->close(); ?> ``` **Logic Breakdown:** | Line | Purpose | Why Important | |------|---------|---------------| | `include 'db.php'` | Reuses connection | DRY principle (Don't Repeat Yourself) | | `$_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"]` | Detects form submission | Prevents direct URL access errors | | `mysqli_real_escape_string()` | Escapes special chars | Basic SQL injection defense | | `password_hash()` | Hashes password using bcrypt | **NEVER store plain text passwords** | | `num_rows > 0` | Checks duplicate email | Data integrity | | `htmlspecialchars()` | Escapes HTML in output | **XSS attack defense** | --- ## 3.5 STEP 5 — Login Page (`login.php`) ```php query($sql); if ($result->num_rows == 1) { $row = $result->fetch_assoc(); if (password_verify($password, $row['password'])) { // Successful login — start session $_SESSION['user_id'] = $row['id']; $_SESSION['user_name']= $row['name']; $_SESSION['reg_no'] = $row['reg_no']; header("Location: dashboard.php"); exit(); } else { $error = "Invalid password!"; } } else { $error = "No user found with that email!"; } } ?> Login

Student Login

$error

"; ?>


``` **Key Concepts:** - `session_start()` → Initializes a PHP session (creates a session ID cookie). - `password_verify()` → Compares plain text with stored hash (constant-time comparison). - `header("Location: ...")` → Redirects to another page. - `exit()` → Stops further script execution after redirect. --- ## 3.6 STEP 6 — Display All Students (`view_students.php`) ```php query($sql); if ($result->num_rows > 0) { echo ""; echo ""; while ($row = $result->fetch_assoc()) { echo ""; } echo "
IDReg NoName EmailDeptRegistered
{$row['id']} {$row['reg_no']} {$row['name']} {$row['email']} {$row['department']} {$row['created_at']}
"; } else { echo "No students registered yet."; } $conn->close(); ?> ``` --- ## 3.7 STEP 7 — Delete Operation (`delete.php`) ```php query($sql) === TRUE) { echo "Record deleted successfully. Rows affected: " . $conn->affected_rows; } else { echo "Error deleting: " . $conn->error; } } header("refresh:2; url=view_students.php"); $conn->close(); ?> ``` --- ## 3.8 STEP 8 — Update Operation (`update.php`) ```php query($sql); $row = $result->fetch_assoc(); } if ($_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"] == "POST") { $name = $_POST['name']; $dept = $_POST['department']; $id = intval($_POST['id']); $sql = "UPDATE students SET name='$name', department='$dept' WHERE id=$id"; if ($conn->query($sql) === TRUE) { echo "Record updated successfully!"; } else { echo "Error: " . $conn->error; } } ?>


``` --- ## 3.9 Analytical Matrix — Code-to-Concept Mapping | Code Snippet | Concept Tested in KTU Exam | Marks Weight | |--------------|-----------------------------|--------------| | `mysqli_real_escape_string()` | SQL Injection Prevention | 2 marks | | `password_hash()` + `password_verify()` | Password Security | 3 marks | | `$_SESSION` + `session_start()` | State Management | 2 marks | | `header("Location: ...")` | Page Redirection | 1 mark | | `fetch_assoc()` in `while` loop | Result Set Iteration | 2 marks | | `affected_rows` / `num_rows` | Operation Verification | 2 marks | | `htmlspecialchars()` | XSS Prevention | 1 mark | | `intval()` for ID parameters | Integer Sanitization | 1 mark | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Architecture Diagram — PHP-MySQL Web Application Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Client Browser] -->|HTTP GET Request| B[Apache Web Server Port 80] B --> C[PHP Engine Interprets .php file] C --> D{Is Database Operation Required?} D -->|No| E[Generate HTML Response] D -->|Yes| F[Open MySQL Connection Port 3306] F --> G[MySQL Database Server] G -->|Query Execution Result| H[PHP Processes Result] H --> E E -->|HTTP Response with HTML| A style A fill:#4F46E5,color:#fff style B fill:#10B981,color:#fff style C fill:#F59E0B,color:#fff style F fill:#EF4444,color:#fff style G fill:#EC4899,color:#fff style H fill:#8B5CF6,color:#fff style E fill:#06B6D4,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.2 CRUD Operations — Complete Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CREATE["C - CREATE"] C1[User fills Form] --> C2[POST to register.php] C2 --> C3[INSERT INTO students] C3 --> C4[New Row Added] end subgraph READ["R - READ"] R1[User views page] --> R2[SELECT query executes] R2 --> R3[fetch_assoc loop] R3 --> R4[HTML Table Displayed] end subgraph UPDATE["U - UPDATE"] U1[User edits form] --> U2[POST to update.php] U2 --> U3[UPDATE students SET] U3 --> U4[Row Modified] end subgraph DELETE["D - DELETE"] D1[User clicks delete] --> D2[GET to delete.php?id=X] D2 --> D3[DELETE FROM students WHERE id=X] D3 --> D4[Row Removed] end style CREATE fill:#DBEAFE style READ fill:#D1FAE5 style UPDATE fill:#FEF3C7 style DELETE fill:#FEE2E2 ``` --- ## 4.3 User Login Authentication Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB Start([User visits login.php]) --> Form[Display Login Form] Form --> Submit{User Submits Form} Submit -->|POST| Query[SELECT from users WHERE email] Query --> Check{Row Found?} Check -->|No| NoUser[Show 'No user found'] Check -->|Yes| Verify{password_verify?} Verify -->|No| WrongPass[Show 'Invalid password'] Verify -->|Yes| CreateSess[Create Session vars] CreateSess --> Redirect[header Location: dashboard.php] Redirect --> End([Dashboard Displayed]) NoUser --> Form WrongPass --> Form style Start fill:#3B82F6,color:#fff style Redirect fill:#10B981,color:#fff style End fill:#10B981,color:#fff style NoUser fill:#EF4444,color:#fff style WrongPass fill:#EF4444,color:#fff ``` --- ## 4.4 PHP-MySQL Connectivity — Two API Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MySQLi["MySQLi Extension MySQL-specific"] M1[Connect: mysqli_connect] M2[Query: mysqli_query] M3[Fetch: mysqli_fetch_assoc] M4[Close: mysqli_close] end subgraph PDO["PDO Extension Multi-database"] P1[Connect: new PDO] P2[Query: query/execute] P3[Fetch: fetch FETCH_ASSOC] P4[Close: null] end App[PHP Application Layer] --> MySQLi App --> PDO MySQLi --> MySQLDB[(MySQL Server Port 3306)] PDO --> MySQLDB PDO --> OtherDB[(PostgreSQL / SQLite / Oracle)] OtherDB -.->|Different driver| PDO style App fill:#6366F1,color:#fff style MySQLi fill:#FCA5A5 style PDO fill:#A7F3D0 style MySQLDB fill:#FCD34D style OtherDB fill:#FCD34D ``` --- ## 4.5 Prepared Statement vs Vulnerable Query (Security) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[User Input: ' OR 1=1 --] --> B{Vulnerable Code} B --> C[String Concatenation: WHERE name='$input'] C --> D[SQL Injection Attack: Returns ALL rows] D --> E[Security Breach] A --> F{Secure Code} F --> G[Prepared Statement: WHERE name=?] G --> H[Input Treated as Data Only] H --> I[Query Returns Correct Result] style E fill:#DC2626,color:#fff style I fill:#16A34A,color:#fff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer) ### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Differentiate between `$_GET` and `$_POST` superglobal arrays in PHP with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | `$_GET` | `$_POST` | |---------|---------|----------| | **Data Visibility** | Visible in URL (e.g., `page.php?id=5`) | Hidden in HTTP body | | **Security** | Less secure (suitable for non-sensitive data) | More secure (used for passwords, etc.) | | **Data Limit** | ~2048 characters (browser-dependent) | 8 MB (configurable in `php.ini`) | | **Bookmarkable** | ✅ Yes (URL contains data) | ❌ No | | **Caching** | Cached by browser | Not cached | | **Example** | Search query: `search.php?q=php+mysql` | Login form submission | ```php // $_GET example $id = $_GET['id']; // URL: view.php?id=10 // $_POST example $user = $_POST['username']; // Sent from form with method="POST" ``` --- ### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** **Explain the role of `password_hash()` and `password_verify()` functions in PHP. Why is it unsafe to store passwords in plain text in MySQL?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** PHP's `password_hash()` uses the **bcrypt algorithm** (a strong, slow, salted hashing algorithm) to convert plain text passwords into a **non-reversible 60-character hash**: ```php $hash = password_hash("MyPassword123", PASSWORD_BCRYPT); // Output: $2y$10$... (60 chars, includes salt and cost factor) ``` `password_verify()` is used during login to **compare a user-entered password with the stored hash** in constant time (prevents timing attacks): ```php if (password_verify($input, $storedHash)) { /* Login success */ } ``` **Why plain text storage is unsafe:** 1. Database breaches expose all user passwords instantly. 2. Insider threats — DBAs can see all passwords. 3. Reuse attacks — users often reuse passwords across sites. 4. **Bcrypt is intentionally slow** (cost factor = 10 → 2¹⁰ = 1024 iterations) to resist brute-force attacks. 5. **Auto-salting** prevents rainbow table attacks. --- ## 📗 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) ### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** **Design and develop a PHP-MySQL web application to implement a "Student Feedback System" for KTU where students can submit feedback about courses, and the administrator can view all feedback entries.** **(a)** Write the SQL commands to create the required database and table. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write the complete PHP code to (i) display a feedback form, (ii) insert feedback into the database, and (iii) display all feedback entries in a tabular format. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution: ### Part (a) — SQL Commands [7 marks] **Step 1: Create Database** ```sql CREATE DATABASE IF NOT EXISTS ktu_feedback; USE ktu_feedback; ``` **Step 2: Create Feedback Table** ```sql CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS feedback ( feedback_id INT(6) UNSIGNED AUTO_INCREMENT PRIMARY KEY, student_name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL, reg_no VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, course_code VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL, rating INT(1) NOT NULL CHECK (rating BETWEEN 1 AND 5), comments TEXT, submitted_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); ``` **Step 3: Insert Sample Data (for testing)** ```sql INSERT INTO feedback (student_name, reg_no, course_code, rating, comments) VALUES ('Arjun Krishnan', 'KTU2023CS001', 'CS301', 5, 'Excellent teaching methodology'), ('Meera Nair', 'KTU2023CS045', 'CS302', 4, 'Good course content'); ``` ### Part (b) — PHP Code [7 marks] **Step 1: Database Connection (`config.php`)** ```php connect_error) { die("Connection failed: " . $conn->connect_error); } ?> ``` **Step 2: Feedback Form + Insertion (`submit_feedback.php`)** ```php real_escape_string($_POST['student_name']); $reg = $conn->real_escape_string($_POST['reg_no']); $course = $conn->real_escape_string($_POST['course_code']); $rating = intval($_POST['rating']); $comment = $conn->real_escape_string($_POST['comments']); $sql = "INSERT INTO feedback (student_name, reg_no, course_code, rating, comments) VALUES ('$name', '$reg', '$course', $rating, '$comment')"; if ($conn->query($sql) === TRUE) { echo "

Feedback submitted successfully!

"; } else { echo "Error: " . $conn->error; } } ?> Submit Feedback - KTU

Course Feedback Form












``` **Step 3: Display All Feedback (`view_feedback.php`)** ```php query($sql); ?> All Feedback - Admin View

All Student Feedback

num_rows > 0) { echo ""; echo ""; while ($row = $result->fetch_assoc()) { echo ""; } echo "
IDNameReg No CourseRatingComments Date
{$row['feedback_id']} " . htmlspecialchars($row['student_name']) . " {$row['reg_no']} {$row['course_code']} {$row['rating']}/5 " . htmlspecialchars($row['comments']) . " {$row['submitted_at']}
"; } else { echo "

No feedback submitted yet.

"; } $conn->close(); ?> ``` --- ### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** **Develop a complete PHP-MySQL "User Registration and Login System" with session management.** **(a)** Explain and demonstrate database connection using MySQLi with proper error handling. Write PHP code to create a registration form and store user credentials securely (using hashing) into MySQL. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Implement login authentication using prepared statements, session creation, and logout functionality. Explain how session variables maintain state across multiple pages. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Model Solution Outline: **Part (a):** - `db.php` with `new mysqli()` + `connect_error` check - `register.html` form with `name`, `email`, `password` fields - `register.php` using `password_hash($_POST['password'], PASSWORD_BCRYPT)` - `INSERT INTO users` with duplicate email check **Part (b):** - `login.php` using **prepared statement**: ```php $stmt = $conn->prepare("SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?"); $stmt->bind_param("s", $email); $stmt->execute(); ``` - `password_verify()` for password comparison - `$_SESSION['user_id'] = $row['id'];` to store state - `dashboard.php` checks `isset($_SESSION['user_id'])` for access control - `logout.php` uses `session_destroy()` to clear session **State Management Explanation:** Sessions work by creating a unique session ID stored in a **PHPSESSID cookie** on the client's browser. The session data itself is stored on the server in temporary files (`/tmp` on Linux). Every subsequent request from the client includes the session ID, allowing PHP to retrieve the associated data via `$_SESSION`. This is why session-based authentication is more secure than query-string-based authentication. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > > | Mistake | Marks Lost | Correct Approach | > |---------|-----------|-----------------| > | Not checking `$_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"] == "POST"` | -1 mark | Always check method to prevent direct URL access | > | Storing passwords in plain text | -2 marks | **Always use `password_hash()` with `PASSWORD_BCRYPT`** | > | Using `$_GET` for login form | -1 mark | Use `$_POST` for sensitive data | > | Missing `htmlspecialchars()` on output | -1 mark | Apply to all user data displayed in HTML (XSS defense) | > | Forgetting to call `session_start()` | -2 marks | Must be called **before any HTML output** in sessions page | > | Not using `intval()` for ID in DELETE/UPDATE | -1 mark | Prevents SQL injection in numeric params | > | Using `mysql_*` functions (deprecated) | -2 marks | Use `mysqli_*` or PDO only | > | Missing `die()` or error handling after `mysqli_connect` | -1 mark | Always check `connect_error` and terminate safely | > | Concatenating user input directly in SQL | -2 marks | Use **prepared statements** OR `real_escape_string()` | > | Not closing connection with `mysqli_close()` | -0.5 mark | Best practice, though PHP auto-closes at script end | --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read Before Every KTU PHP-MySQL Exam** ### 🧠 Must-Know Concepts - ✅ PHP is **server-side**, executes on the web server, output is sent as HTML to the browser. - ✅ MySQLi has two styles: **Procedural** (`mysqli_connect`) and **Object-Oriented** (`new mysqli`). - ✅ PDO supports **multiple database systems** with one consistent API. - ✅ Connection requires **4 parameters**: host, username, password, database. - ✅ Default XAMPP credentials: user = `root`, password = `""` (empty). - ✅ MySQL runs on **port 3306**, Apache on **port 80**. - ✅ `localhost` = `127.0.0.1` (loopback IP address). ### 🛠️ Must-Know Functions | Function | Purpose | |----------|---------| | `mysqli_connect()` / `new mysqli()` | Open connection | | `mysqli_query()` / `$conn->query()` | Execute SQL | | `mysqli_fetch_assoc()` | Fetch one row as array | | `mysqli_num_rows()` | Count rows in result | | `mysqli_affected_rows()` | Count modified rows | | `mysqli_real_escape_string()` | Sanitize input | | `password_hash()` / `password_verify()` | Secure passwords | | `session_start()` / `session_destroy()` | Manage sessions | | `header("Location: ...")` | Redirect | | `htmlspecialchars()` | XSS prevention | ### 🔐 Security Rules (Lose Marks If You Forget) 1. **Always use `password_hash()` and `password_verify()`** — never MD5, SHA1, or plain text. 2. **Always use prepared statements OR `real_escape_string()`** — SQL injection is a top web vulnerability. 3. **Always use `htmlspecialchars()` on output** — prevent XSS attacks. 4. **Use `intval()` for numeric URL parameters** — extra safety for IDs. 5. **Always check `$_SERVER["REQUEST_METHOD"]`** in form processing scripts. 6. **Start session with `session_start()` BEFORE any HTML output.** 7. **Validate on both client (`required`, `type="email"`) and server side.** ### 📂 Standard Folder Structure ``` htdocs/ └── ktu_lab/ ├── db.php (connection) ├── config.php (alternate connection file) ├── register.html (form page) ├── register.php (insert handler) ├── login.php (login + session) ├── dashboard.php (protected page) ├── logout.php (session destroy) ├── view_students.php (SELECT display) ├── update.php (UPDATE handler) └── delete.php (DELETE handler) ``` ### ⚙️ Common XAMPP Commands | Action | Path / Command | |--------|---------------| | Start Apache & MySQL | XAMPP Control Panel → Click "Start" | | Access phpMyAdmin | `http://localhost/phpmyadmin` | | Run a PHP file | `http://localhost/ktu_lab/register.php` | | Default htdocs location | `C:\xampp\htdocs\` (Windows) | | Check PHP version | Run `php -v` in terminal | ### 🎯 The 4 Magic Words of Database Design | Word | SQL | HTTP Method | When to Use | |------|-----|-------------|-------------| | **C**reate | `INSERT` | POST | New records | | **R**ead | `SELECT` | GET | Display records | | **U**pdate | `UPDATE` | POST/PUT | Modify records | | **D**elete | `DELETE` | POST/DELETE | Remove records | > **Final Tip:** In your KTU lab exam, **always explain your code line-by-line** in the observation book. The examiner awards marks for **understanding**, not just working output. Show ER diagrams, table structures, and use **proper indentation** in code.

topics.title: Develop and execute Client-side and Server-side scripts using CSS, Javascript and PHP
Develop Web Applications using Laravel framework
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCNL507 - WEB PROGRAMMING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Develop Web Applications using Laravel framework # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Laravel** is an **open-source, server-side PHP web application framework** created by **Taylor Otwell** (initial release: **June 2011**) that follows the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** architectural pattern. It provides an elegant syntax, expressive ORM (Eloquent), powerful routing engine, templating engine (Blade), and built-in tools for caching, authentication, queueing, and testing, making it the de-facto standard for enterprise-grade PHP development. For the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCNL507 – Web Programming Lab), the **Module Experiment 2** mandates that students **develop a fully functional dynamic web application** using the Laravel framework. This involves demonstrating mastery over the **MVC pipeline** (Route $\rightarrow$ Controller $\rightarrow$ View), **database interaction via Eloquent ORM**, **Blade templating**, and **CRUD (Create, Read, Update, Delete)** operations. | Term | Meaning in Laravel Context | |---|---| | **Framework** | A reusable, opinionated software skeleton providing structure, libraries, and conventions. | | **MVC** | Design pattern separating **Model** (data), **View** (UI), **Controller** (logic). | | **Composer** | PHP dependency manager that installs Laravel and its packages. | | **Artisan** | Laravel's **Command-Line Interface (CLI)** for scaffolding, migrations, and automation. | | **Eloquent ORM** | Laravel's **ActiveRecord-based Object-Relational Mapper** for database interaction. | | **Blade** | Laravel's lightweight **server-side templating engine** using `.blade.php` files. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Laravel is Like a Professional Restaurant Kitchen" Imagine a **busy restaurant**: - **The Customer (Browser)** places an order (HTTP request) at the counter. - **The Waiter (Router)** receives the order and knows exactly which chef to call. - **The Head Chef (Controller)** decides what dishes to prepare, fetches ingredients (data from Model), and coordinates the plating. - **The Pantry (Database via Eloquent Model)** stores and retrieves all ingredients. - **The Plating Station (Blade View / Template)** beautifully arranges the dish for presentation to the customer. - **The Restaurant Manager (Artisan CLI)** handles prep work, stock-taking, and quality checks. > [!NOTE] > **Just as a restaurant would collapse without a structured kitchen, a web application without a framework becomes messy, insecure, and unscalable.** Laravel provides the **structured kitchen** so you (the developer) can focus on **cooking great applications**. ## 1.3 Why Laravel is the KTU-Preferred Framework - **Elegant syntax** $\rightarrow$ Lowers the learning curve for B.Tech students. - **MVC discipline** $\rightarrow$ Aligns with KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) COs requiring separation of concerns. - **Built-in security** (CSRF tokens, hashed passwords, SQL injection prevention via Eloquent). - **Rich ecosystem** (Laravel Breeze, Jetstream, Sanctum, Livewire, Inertia.js). - **Industry adoption** — Used by companies like 9GAG, Pfizer, BBC, and the majority of PHP-based startups. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Learning Outcome Mapping:** > - **CO1** $\rightarrow$ Understand PHP framework fundamentals. > - **CO2** $\rightarrow$ Develop dynamic web applications with database integration. > - **CO3** $\rightarrow$ Implement MVC architecture for maintainable code. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Laravel Request-Response Lifecycle (The "Why" Behind Each Step) The lifecycle describes **how an HTTP request travels through Laravel's core and returns a response**. Understanding this is critical for KTU viva questions. | # | Step | Component | "Why" It Exists | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Entry Point | `public/index.php` | Single front controller; bootstraps the framework. | | 2 | Bootstrap | `bootstrap/app.php` | Creates the application service container. | | 3 | HTTP Kernel | `App\Http\Kernel` | Registers middleware and handles the request. | | 4 | Routing | `routes/web.php` / `routes/api.php` | Maps the URL to a closure or controller action. | | 5 | Middleware Pipeline | Authentication, CSRF, CORS | Filters requests before reaching the controller. | | 6 | Controller | `App\Http\Controllers` | Executes business logic and prepares the response. | | 7 | Eloquent ORM / Model | `App\Models` | Interacts with the database using PHP objects. | | 8 | View / Blade | `resources/views/*.blade.php` | Renders the final HTML response. | | 9 | Response $\rightarrow$ Browser | HTTP Response | Sent back through the middleware chain. | ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Laravel Components | Component | File/Folder | Purpose | Key Command | |---|---|---|---| | **Route** | `routes/web.php` | URL $\rightarrow$ Controller mapping | `Route::get('/url', [Ctrl::class, 'method'])` | | **Controller** | `app/Http/Controllers/` | Business logic | `php artisan make:controller NameController` | | **Model** | `app/Models/` | Database abstraction (Eloquent) | `php artisan make:model Student -m` | | **Migration** | `database/migrations/` | Schema version control | `php artisan make:migration create_table_name` | | **Seeder** | `database/seeders/` | Sample data insertion | `php artisan make:seeder StudentSeeder` | | **View (Blade)** | `resources/views/` | UI rendering (`.blade.php`) | `php artisan make:view student.index` | | **Middleware** | `app/Http/Middleware/` | Request filtering | `php artisan make:middleware CheckAge` | | **Request Validation** | `app/Http/Requests/` | Form validation rules | `php artisan make:request StoreStudentRequest` | | **Form Request** | `app/Http/Requests/` | Type-safe input | `php artisan make:request Xyz` | | **Resource Controller** | Auto-routes 7 RESTful methods | `Route::resource('students', StudentController::class)` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip — Memorize the 7 RESTful Resource Routes** (this is a guaranteed 3-mark question): > `index` $\rightarrow$ `create` $\rightarrow$ `store` $\rightarrow$ `show` $\rightarrow$ `edit` $\rightarrow$ `update` $\rightarrow$ `destroy` ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Blade Syntax Essentials | Blade Directive | PHP Equivalent | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `{{ $var }}` | `` | Escaped output (XSS-safe) | | `{!! $html !!}` | `` | Unescaped output (use with caution) | | `@if / @else / @endif` | `if/else` | Conditional rendering | | `@foreach / @endforeach` | `foreach` | Loop over collection | | `@csrf` | Hidden CSRF token input | Security (mandatory in forms) | | `@method('PUT')` | Hidden HTTP verb spoofing | RESTful PUT/PATCH/DELETE | | `@yield('section')` | `yieldContent('section'); ?>` | Defines child-injectable slot | | `@extends('layout')` | Template inheritance | Inherits parent Blade layout | | `@section('content')` | Defines a yield block | Child content block | | `@include('partial.name')` | Imports a sub-view | Component reuse | ## 2.4 Real-World Utility | Domain | Laravel Application Example | |---|---| | **E-Commerce** | Shopping cart, payment gateways (Stripe, Razorpay) | | **Education** | KTU student portals, LMS, attendance systems | | **Healthcare** | Patient records, appointment booking | | **Banking/FinTech** | Loan management, KYC, transaction logs | | **SaaS** | Multi-tenant dashboards, subscription billing | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Implementation & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > This section walks through the **complete end-to-end construction of a "Student Management System"** — the most commonly asked Lab Exam experiment in KTU 2024. Every line of code is provided with explanatory text. ## 3.1 Step 1 — Install Prerequisites & Create Laravel Project **Why:** Laravel requires PHP 8.1+, Composer, and a database. The framework is delivered as a Composer package. ### Prerequisites Check ```bash php --version # Must be >= 8.1 composer --version # Must be >= 2.x mysql --version # Any recent version ``` ### Create New Laravel Project ```bash composer create-project laravel/laravel student-management cd student-management php artisan serve # Starts server at http://127.0.0.1:8000 ``` **Analytical Matrix — What `composer create-project` does:** | Phase | Action | |---|---| | 1 | Downloads Laravel skeleton from Packagist. | | 2 | Installs all dependencies declared in `composer.json`. | | 3 | Generates `.env` file with default app key. | | 4 | Sets up `vendor/` directory with PHP packages. | ## 3.2 Step 2 — Configure Database (`.env` file) **Why:** Laravel must know the database credentials before migrations run. ```ini DB_CONNECTION=mysql DB_HOST=127.0.0.1 DB_PORT=3306 DB_DATABASE=student_db DB_USERNAME=root DB_PASSWORD= ``` Create the database in MySQL: ```sql CREATE DATABASE student_db; ``` ## 3.3 Step 3 — Create Model + Migration + Controller (One Artisan Call) **Why:** The `-mcr` flag (model + controller + resource) ensures we get a **Resourceful Controller** (with all 7 RESTful methods pre-generated). ```bash php artisan make:model Student -mcr ``` This single command creates: - `app/Models/Student.php` - `database/migrations/xxxx_create_students_table.php` - `app/Http/Controllers/StudentController.php` (with `index`, `create`, `store`, `show`, `edit`, `update`, `destroy`) ## 3.4 Step 4 — Define Migration Schema **Why:** Migrations are **version-controlled schema definitions** that let you recreate the database structure anywhere. **File:** `database/migrations/2024_xx_xx_create_students_table.php` ```php id(); // Auto-increment primary key $table->string('name'); // VARCHAR(255) $table->string('email')->unique(); // Unique email constraint $table->string('course'); // e.g., "B.Tech CSE" $table->integer('semester')->default(1); $table->timestamps(); // created_at, updated_at }); } public function down(): void { Schema::dropIfExists('students'); } }; ``` **Run the migration to create the actual table:** ```bash php artisan migrate ``` **Analytical Matrix — Migration Lifecycle:** | Command | Effect | |---|---| | `php artisan migrate` | Runs all pending migrations. | | `php artisan migrate:rollback` | Reverses the last batch. | | `php artisan migrate:fresh` | Drops ALL tables and re-runs. | | `php artisan migrate:status` | Lists which migrations ran. | ## 3.5 Step 5 — Define Routes (Step 1 of MVC Pipeline) **Why:** The router is the **traffic controller** — it maps URLs to controllers. **File:** `routes/web.php` ```php validate([ 'name' => 'required|string|max:255', 'email' => 'required|email|unique:students,email', 'course' => 'required|string|max:100', 'semester' => 'required|integer|between:1,8', ]); Student::create($request->all()); return redirect()->route('students.index') ->with('success', 'Student added successfully!'); } // Display a single student public function show(Student $student) { return view('students.show', compact('student')); } // Show edit form public function edit(Student $student) { return view('students.edit', compact('student')); } // Update existing student public function update(Request $request, Student $student) { $request->validate([ 'name' => 'required|string|max:255', 'email' => 'required|email|unique:students,email,' . $student->id, 'course' => 'required|string|max:100', 'semester' => 'required|integer|between:1,8', ]); $student->update($request->all()); return redirect()->route('students.index') ->with('success', 'Student updated successfully!'); } // Delete student public function destroy(Student $student) { $student->delete(); return redirect()->route('students.index') ->with('success', 'Student deleted successfully!'); } } ``` **Analytical Matrix — Eloquent Common Operations:** | Operation | Eloquent Syntax | |---|---| | Retrieve all | `Student::all()` | | Find by ID | `Student::find($id)` | | Conditional | `Student::where('course', 'B.Tech CSE')->get()` | | Insert | `Student::create($data)` | | Update | `$student->update($data)` | | Delete | `$student->delete()` | | Pagination | `Student::paginate(10)` | | Order | `Student::orderBy('name', 'asc')->get()` | | Count | `Student::count()` | ## 3.8 Step 8 — Blade Views (Step 4 of MVC Pipeline — Presentation) ### 3.8.1 Master Layout — `resources/views/layouts/app.blade.php` **Why:** Master layouts enforce **DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself)** — all child pages inherit the same navbar/footer. ```html @yield('title', 'Student Management')
🎓 KTU Student Portal
@if(session('success'))
{{ session('success') }}
@endif @yield('content')
``` ### 3.8.2 Index View — `resources/views/students/index.blade.php` ```html @extends('layouts.app') @section('title', 'All Students') @section('content')

Student List

+ Add New Student
@forelse($students as $student) @empty @endforelse
IDNameEmail CourseSemesterActions
{{ $student->id }} {{ $student->name }} {{ $student->email }} {{ $student->course }} {{ $student->semester }} View Edit
@csrf @method('DELETE')
No students found.
@endsection ``` ### 3.8.3 Create View — `resources/views/students/create.blade.php` ```html @extends('layouts.app') @section('title', 'Add Student') @section('content')

Add New Student

@csrf
@error('name') {{ $message }} @enderror
@error('email') {{ $message }} @enderror
@error('course') {{ $message }} @enderror
@error('semester') {{ $message }} @enderror
Cancel
@endsection ``` ### 3.8.4 Edit View — `resources/views/students/edit.blade.php` ```html @extends('layouts.app') @section('title', 'Edit Student') @section('content')

Edit Student — {{ $student->name }}

@csrf @method('PUT')
@error('name') {{ $message }} @enderror
@error('email') {{ $message }} @enderror
Cancel
@endsection ``` ### 3.8.5 Show View — `resources/views/students/show.blade.php` ```html @extends('layouts.app') @section('title', 'Student Details') @section('content')

Student Details

ID{{ $student->id }}
Name{{ $student->name }}
Email{{ $student->email }}
Course{{ $student->course }}
Semester{{ $student->semester }}
Created{{ $student->created_at->format('d-M-Y H:i') }}
Back Edit @endsection ``` ## 3.9 Step 9 — Verification & End-to-End Test Flow **Analytical Matrix — Test the Full CRUD Pipeline:** | Step | Action | Expected Outcome | |---|---|---| | 1 | Visit `/students` | Empty table with "Add New Student" button. | | 2 | Click "Add New Student" | Form rendered via `students.create`. | | 3 | Submit valid form | Redirect to `/students` with success flash. | | 4 | Submit invalid form | Inline error messages shown. | | 5 | Click "Edit" | Pre-filled form via `students.edit`. | | 6 | Submit update | Row updated, redirect to index. | | 7 | Click "Delete" | Row removed with confirmation. | | 8 | Click "View" | Single student details rendered. | ## 3.10 Step 10 — Add a Seeder for Sample Data (Optional but Recommended for Lab) ```bash php artisan make:seeder StudentSeeder ``` **File:** `database/seeders/StudentSeeder.php` ```php 'Ananya Krishna', 'email' => 'ananya@ktu.ac.in', 'course' => 'B.Tech CSE', 'semester' => 5, ]); Student::create([ 'name' => 'Rahul Menon', 'email' => 'rahul@ktu.ac.in', 'course' => 'B.Tech ECE', 'semester' => 3, ]); } } ``` Run the seeder: ```bash php artisan db:seed --class=StudentSeeder ``` # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Laravel MVC Request-Response Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Browser Client] -->|HTTP GET /students| B[public/index.php Entry Point] B --> C[HTTP Kernel Bootstrap] C --> D[Service Container] D --> E[Route File: routes/web.php] E --> F{Middleware Pipeline} F -->|Auth, CSRF Check| G[StudentController@index] G --> H[Eloquent Model: Student] H -->|SQL Query| I[(MySQL Database)] I -->|Result Set| H H -->|Collection of Students| G G --> J[Blade View: students.index.blade.php] J -->|Rendered HTML| F F -->|HTTP Response| A ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Laravel Folder Structure & Component Roles ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Root A1[app/] --> A2[Http/Controllers/StudentController.php] A1 --> A3[Models/Student.php] A1 --> A4[Http/Middleware/] B1[routes/] --> B2[web.php] B1 --> B3[api.php] C1[database/] --> C2[migrations/] C1 --> C3[seeders/] D1[resources/] --> D2[views/layouts/app.blade.php] D1 --> D3[views/students/] E1[public/] --> E2[index.php] F1[config/] --> F2[app.php, database.php] G1[.env] --> G2[DB Credentials, App Key] end ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — CRUD Resource Mapping ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Route::resource students] --> R2[GET /students] R1 --> R3[GET /students/create] R1 --> R4[POST /students] R1 --> R5[GET /students/123] R1 --> R6[GET /students/123/edit] R1 --> R7[PUT /students/123] R1 --> R8[DELETE /students/123] R2 --> M1[index: List All] R3 --> M2[create: Show Form] R4 --> M3[store: Save New] R5 --> M4[show: Display One] R6 --> M5[edit: Show Edit Form] R7 --> M6[update: Apply Changes] R8 --> M7[destroy: Remove Record] ``` ## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Blade Template Inheritance ```mermaid flowchart TD P[layouts/app.blade.php
Master Layout] --> Y1[@yield content] P --> Y2[@yield title] P --> C1[students/index.blade.php] P --> C2[students/create.blade.php] P --> C3[students/edit.blade.php] P --> C4[students/show.blade.php] C1 --> S1[@extends layouts.app] C1 --> S2[@section content ... @endsection] C2 --> S1 C2 --> S3[@section content ... @endsection] C3 --> S1 C3 --> S4[@section content ... @endsection] C4 --> S1 C4 --> S5[@section content ... @endsection] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Lab Exam Tip — Always carry a hand-drawn version of the above MVC diagram on the first page of your answer booklet. Examiners reward visual clarity in Lab exams.** # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define Laravel framework. List any four features of Laravel.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Laravel is an **open-source PHP web application framework** that follows the **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** architectural pattern. It was created by **Taylor Otwell** and is used to build robust, scalable, and maintainable web applications using expressive, elegant syntax. **Four Key Features:** 1. **MVC Architecture** — Clean separation of data, UI, and logic. 2. **Eloquent ORM** — Database interaction using PHP objects (no raw SQL). 3. **Blade Templating Engine** — Lightweight, powerful, with template inheritance. 4. **Artisan CLI** — Automates repetitive tasks like migrations, seeding, and scaffolding. > *(1 mark for definition + 0.5 mark each × 4 features = 3 marks)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the role of `.env` file in Laravel? What is `php artisan`?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **`.env` file:** Stores environment-specific configuration such as **database credentials** (DB_HOST, DB_USERNAME, DB_PASSWORD), **app key** (APP_KEY), **mail settings**, and **cache drivers**. It is loaded at runtime and overrides defaults in the `config/` directory. This enables the same code to run across development, staging, and production without modification. - **`php artisan`:** Artisan is Laravel's built-in **Command-Line Interface (CLI)** that provides commands for: - `php artisan serve` (run dev server) - `php artisan make:controller / make:model / make:migration` - `php artisan migrate / db:seed / migrate:rollback` - `php artisan route:list` (view all routes) > *(1.5 marks for `.env` + 1.5 marks for Artisan = 3 marks)* --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by: > - Forgetting to add the **`$fillable` array** in the Model (mass-assignment exception). > - Missing `@csrf` token in forms, leading to `419 Page Expired` errors. > - Using `@method('PUT')` in the update form — it is **mandatory** for RESTful updates. > - Hardcoding URLs like `/students/1` instead of using `route('students.show', $id)` helpers. > - Forgetting to run `php artisan migrate` before testing the application. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **MVC architecture in Laravel** with a neat diagram. How does Laravel implement the separation of concerns? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Develop a Laravel application to perform **CRUD operations** on a `Product` table with fields: `id`, `name`, `price`, `description`, `stock`. Show the migration, model, controller, and routes. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Question A (a): **MVC Architecture in Laravel** (7 Marks) The **Model-View-Controller (MVC)** pattern divides application logic into **three interconnected components** to ensure modularity, maintainability, and testability. **Diagram:** ``` ┌────────────────────┐ │ Browser Request │ └────────┬───────────┘ │ ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ │ Route (Router) │ ← maps URL to Controller └────────┬───────────┘ │ ▼ ┌────────────────────┐ │ Controller Logic │ ← orchestrates Model + View └──┬──────────────┬──┘ │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ Model (DB) │ │ View (Blade) │ │ Eloquent │ │ .blade.php │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ │ │ ▼ ▼ ┌──────────────┐ ┌──────────────┐ │ Database │ │ HTML Output │ └──────────────┘ └──────────────┘ ``` **Separation of Concerns:** | Layer | Folder | Responsibility | Example File | |---|---|---|---| | **Model** | `app/Models/` | Data logic, DB queries via Eloquent | `Student.php` | | **View** | `resources/views/` | UI rendering only (no logic) | `students/index.blade.php` | | **Controller** | `app/Http/Controllers/` | Receives request, calls Model, returns View | `StudentController.php` | **Benefits:** Code reusability, parallel development by teams, easier debugging, and clean testing of each layer independently. --- #### Solution to Question A (b): **Product CRUD Implementation** (7 Marks) **Step 1 — Create model, migration, controller (resourceful):** ```bash php artisan make:model Product -mcr ``` **Step 2 — Migration `database/migrations/xxxx_create_products_table.php`:** ```php Schema::create('products', function (Blueprint $table) { $table->id(); $table->string('name'); $table->decimal('price', 10, 2); $table->text('description')->nullable(); $table->integer('stock')->default(0); $table->timestamps(); }); ``` Run: `php artisan migrate` **Step 3 — Model `app/Models/Product.php`:** ```php class Product extends Model { protected $fillable = ['name', 'price', 'description', 'stock']; } ``` **Step 4 — Controller `app/Http/Controllers/ProductController.php`:** ```php class ProductController extends Controller { public function index() { $products = Product::all(); return view('products.index', compact('products')); } public function create() { return view('products.create'); } public function store(Request $request) { $request->validate([ 'name' => 'required', 'price' => 'required|numeric|min:0', 'stock' => 'required|integer|min:0', ]); Product::create($request->all()); return redirect()->route('products.index')->with('success', 'Product added!'); } public function show(Product $product) { return view('products.show', compact('product')); } public function edit(Product $product) { return view('products.edit', compact('product')); } public function update(Request $request, Product $product) { $request->validate([ 'name' => 'required', 'price' => 'required|numeric', 'stock' => 'required|integer', ]); $product->update($request->all()); return redirect()->route('products.index')->with('success', 'Updated!'); } public function destroy(Product $product) { $product->delete(); return redirect()->route('products.index')->with('success', 'Deleted!'); } } ``` **Step 5 — Routes `routes/web.php`:** ```php use App\Http\Controllers\ProductController; Route::resource('products', ProductController::class); ``` This single `Route::resource` line auto-generates **all 7 RESTful routes** required for CRUD. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** What are **Blade templates**? Explain template inheritance with `@extends`, `@section`, and `@yield` using a suitable example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the role of **Eloquent ORM** in Laravel. Write a controller to fetch all students whose semester is greater than 4, and pass the data to a Blade view. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Solution to Question B (a): **Blade Templates** (7 Marks) **Blade** is Laravel's **server-side templating engine** that allows writing PHP and HTML together with clean, readable syntax. All Blade files end with `.blade.php` and are stored in `resources/views/`. **Template Inheritance — Three Key Directives:** | Directive | Purpose | Located In | |---|---|---| | `@yield('name')` | Defines a **placeholder slot** in master layout | Master (parent) | | `@section('name') ... @endsection` | **Fills the slot** with child content | Child view | | `@extends('layout')` | Declares which master to inherit from | Child view (first line) | **Example:** **Master Layout — `resources/views/layouts/master.blade.php`:** ```html @yield('title', 'Default Title')

KTU Portal

@yield('content')
© 2024 KTU
``` **Child View — `resources/views/home.blade.php`:** ```html @extends('layouts.master') @section('title', 'Home Page') @section('content')

Welcome to KTU

This is the home page content.

@endsection ``` **Benefits:** Reusable layout, consistent UI, no code duplication, and clean separation of structure and content. --- #### Solution to Question B (b): **Eloquent ORM** (7 Marks) **Eloquent ORM (Object-Relational Mapper)** is Laravel's built-in data access layer that lets developers **interact with database tables as PHP objects** — each model class maps to a table, and each instance maps to a row. It eliminates raw SQL queries and provides an expressive, ActiveRecord-style API. **Advantages:** - No SQL injection vulnerabilities (uses PDO parameter binding). - Built-in relationships (hasMany, belongsTo, belongsToMany). - Mass-assignment safety via `$fillable` and `$guarded`. - Pagination, soft deletes, events, observers supported. **Controller Code — `app/Http/Controllers/StudentController.php`:** ```php 4 using Eloquent query builder $seniorStudents = Student::where('semester', '>', 4) ->orderBy('semester', 'asc') ->get(); // Pass data to Blade view return view('students.senior', compact('seniorStudents')); } } ``` **Route — `routes/web.php`:** ```php Route::get('/students/senior', [StudentController::class, 'seniorStudents']) ->name('students.senior'); ``` **Blade View — `resources/views/students/senior.blade.php`:** ```html @extends('layouts.app') @section('content')

Senior Students (Semester > 4)

@forelse($seniorStudents as $student) @empty @endforelse
NameCourseSemester
{{ $student->name }} {{ $student->course }} {{ $student->semester }}
No senior students found.
@endsection ``` --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Lab Exam (PCCNL507):** - ⭐ Laravel is a **PHP MVC framework** created by **Taylor Otwell (2011)**. - ⭐ Install via `composer create-project laravel/laravel project-name`. - ⭐ Start dev server: `php artisan serve` $\rightarrow$ runs at `http://127.0.0.1:8000`. - ⭐ **Folder structure:** `app/` (Models/Controllers), `routes/`, `database/`, `resources/views/`, `public/`, `.env`. - ⭐ **Artisan CLI** is the command-line tool for scaffolding (`make:controller`, `make:model -mcr`). - ⭐ **Migrations** = version-controlled schema files; run with `php artisan migrate`. - ⭐ **Eloquent ORM** = each model maps to a DB table; use `$fillable` for mass-assignment safety. - ⭐ **Resourceful routes** auto-generate 7 RESTful methods: `index, create, store, show, edit, update, destroy`. - ⭐ **Blade** uses `.blade.php` files; key directives: `@yield`, `@section`, `@extends`, `@csrf`, `@method`, `@if`, `@foreach`. - ⭐ **`@csrf` is mandatory** in all POST/PUT/DELETE forms to prevent Cross-Site Request Forgery. - ⭐ **`@method('PUT')`** is required when updating via HTML form (HTML only supports GET/POST). - ⭐ Use `{{ }}` for **escaped** output (XSS-safe); use `{!! !!}` for **unescaped** (dangerous). - ⭐ Validation: `$request->validate([...])` with rules like `required`, `email`, `unique:table,column`, `integer`, `min:max`. - ⭐ Common Eloquent methods: `all()`, `find($id)`, `where()`, `create()`, `update()`, `delete()`, `paginate()`, `orderBy()`. - ⭐ `Route::resource('name', Controller::class)` — single line creates all 7 CRUD routes. - ⭐ `compact('var')` passes variables to views; equivalent to `['var' => $var]`. - ⭐ Use `route('name', $id)` helper instead of hardcoding URLs. - ⭐ Flash messages via `with('success', 'message')` and displayed with `@if(session('success'))`. - ⭐ **Common Exam Mistakes:** missing `$fillable`, missing `@csrf`, missing `@method('PUT')` in edit forms, forgetting `php artisan migrate`. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Lab Tip — Practice the full CRUD flow at least 3 times before the lab exam. Most marks are awarded for a working application + clean Blade templates, not just theory. Carry a printed cheat-sheet of Artisan commands and Blade directives to your lab slot.**
